Name: rate-distortion_theoretic_model_compression
Title: Rate-Distortion Theoretic Model Compression: Successive Refinement for Pruning
Short Hypothesis: Model compression can be framed as a successive refinement problem in rate-distortion theory, where each pruning step optimally trades off distortion (task performance) for rate (model size), leading to a principled and interpretable compression strategy.
Related Work: Existing model compression methods often rely on heuristic pruning strategies or quantization techniques without a clear theoretical foundation. Recent work in rate-distortion theory, such as Sefidgaran et al. (2022), has begun to bridge this gap by applying information-theoretic principles to generalization bounds. However, these approaches have not yet been extended to the specific problem of model pruning. Our proposal distinguishes itself by framing pruning as a successive refinement process, where each step is guided by rate-distortion trade-offs, providing a theoretically grounded and interpretable method for model compression.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to model compression based on rate-distortion theory, treating pruning as a successive refinement problem. Each pruning step is optimized to trade off distortion (task performance) for rate (model size), ensuring that the compressed model remains as effective as possible for the given resource constraints. By leveraging information-theoretic principles, our method provides a principled and interpretable strategy for model compression, grounded in rigorous mathematical theory. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, demonstrating that our method achieves superior compression-performance trade-offs compared to existing heuristic-based pruning strategies. Our results highlight the potential of rate-distortion theory to inform and improve model compression techniques, offering a new direction for research at the intersection of information theory and machine learning.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Successive Pruning on CIFAR-10. We apply our rate-distortion theoretic pruning method to a ResNet-18 model trained on CIFAR-10. We measure the trade-off between model size (rate) and classification accuracy (distortion) at each pruning step, comparing our method to traditional magnitude-based pruning.

Experiment 2: Generalization on ImageNet. We evaluate the generalization performance of a pruned ResNet-50 model on ImageNet, comparing our method to state-of-the-art pruning techniques. We report top-1 and top-5 accuracy as well as the compression ratio achieved.

Experiment 3: Robustness to Distribution Shifts. We test the robustness of pruned models on the WILDS benchmark, measuring performance under natural distribution shifts. We compare our method to heuristic pruning strategies, focusing on both accuracy and compression efficiency.

Experiment 4: Theoretical Validation. We derive and validate theoretical bounds on the rate-distortion trade-off for our pruning method, comparing empirical results to our theoretical predictions. We use mutual information and Fisher information as proxies for task-relevant information.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Theoretical Assumptions: Our approach relies on certain assumptions about the distribution of model weights and their impact on task performance. Deviations from these assumptions could affect the effectiveness of our method.

Computational Overhead: Calculating rate-distortion trade-offs at each pruning step may introduce additional computational overhead, potentially limiting the scalability of our method to very large models.
Generalization to Other Tasks: While our experiments focus on image classification, the effectiveness of our method on other tasks (e.g., natural language processing) remains to be validated.
Interpretability vs. Performance: While our method offers interpretable pruning decisions, there may be a trade-off between interpretability and absolute performance compared to heuristic methods.
